subject: First Aid & Safety
title: Frostbite Science
description: A micro-lesson that makes Frostbite Science usable.

hook.text: Frostbite forms ice crystals inside your cells. They puncture cell membranes from the inside out.

buildup.text: Fingers, toes, ears, and nose lose heat fastest—blood retreats to protect the vital core.

discovery.text: Frostbitten skin turns white and waxy, then numb. The lack of pain is a dangerous false comfort.

twist.text: Rewarming frostbite is agonizingly painful—nerve cells fire as blood returns. But refreezing is far worse.

climax.text: Never rub frostbitten skin or use direct heat—thaw slowly in warm water (100-104°F).

punchline.text: Ice crystals inside your cells. That's frostbite. Thaw slowly in warm water.

quiz.question: Why is rubbing frostbitten skin harmful?
options: Ice crystals in cells cause more tissue damage when disturbed
It causes the skin to blister
It makes the person too warm too fast
